diseases.name: Common Cold
diseases.description: A viral infection of the upper respiratory tract (usually mild) that commonly causes a runny nose, congestion, sore throat, and cough.
precautions: Rest and drink plenty of fluids.
Stay hydrated; consider warm teas or soups.
Use saline nasal spray or gentle steam inhalation for congestion.
Practice good hand hygiene and avoid close contact to reduce spread.
Seek care if symptoms worsen or persist beyond about 7-10 days.

otc_medicines.name: Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) for fever/pain
otc_medicines.how_often: Every 4-6 hours as needed (adults) - follow the package label for exact dosing.
dosage_guidelines: Adults: follow the package label for dosing intervals.
Typical adult max is often 3000 mg/day (some products differ); do not exceed the label maximum.
Avoid if you have severe liver disease or if directed by a clinician.

otc_medicines.name: Ibuprofen for fever/pain (NSAID)
otc_medicines.how_often: Every 6-8 hours as needed (adults) - follow the package label for exact dosing.
dosage_guidelines: Adults: follow the package label.
Typical OTC max is often 1200 mg/day.
Avoid if you have a history of stomach ulcers/bleeding, kidney disease, are dehydrated, or are advised not to take NSAIDs.

otc_medicines.name: Saline nasal spray / saline rinse
otc_medicines.how_often: As directed on the label (often several times daily) - follow exact usage instructions.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the product label.
Helpful for congestion without systemic drug effects.
Use only as directed on the label.
Do not combine multiple products that contain the same ingredient (for example, many cold/flu products include acetaminophen).
If symptoms are mild, consider starting with non-medication measures like rest and fluids.
when_to_consult_doctor: Shortness of breath, chest pain, or bluish lips.
Fever lasting more than 3 days or very high fever.
Severe sore throat, symptoms rapidly worsening, or new ear pain/sinus swelling.
Symptoms that persist beyond 7-10 days without improvement.

diseases.name: Influenza (Flu)
diseases.description: A viral respiratory illness that often comes on suddenly and can cause fever, chills, body aches, fatigue, and cough.
precautions: Avoid spreading infection (masks in shared spaces, hand hygiene).
If you are high-risk (pregnancy, age extremes, chronic illnesses), contact a clinician early.

otc_medicines.name: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen for fever and aches
otc_medicines.how_often: Choose ONE: acetaminophen every 4-6 hours as needed OR ibuprofen every 6-8 hours as needed - follow the package label.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the package label.
Do not exceed the label maximum daily dose.
Avoid NSAIDs if you have specific contraindications (ulcer history, kidney disease, dehydration).

otc_medicines.name: Throat lozenges / warm saltwater gargles
otc_medicines.how_often: Lozenges: every 2-3 hours as needed (follow label). Gargles: repeat as needed for comfort.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed; repeat as needed for symptom relief.
Warm saltwater gargles can soothe throat irritation.
Consider treating fever and discomfort rather than trying to 'cure' with OTC medicines.
Stay cautious with multi-symptom cold/flu products to prevent ingredient duplication.
when_to_consult_doctor: Trouble breathing, chest pain, or confusion.
Dehydration (very dry mouth, dizziness, minimal urination).
Symptoms improving then suddenly worsening.
High-risk conditions (pregnancy, immunocompromised status, chronic lung/heart/kidney disease) especially if symptoms are within the first couple of days.

diseases.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Seasonal Allergies)
diseases.description: An allergy-related inflammation of the nose and sometimes eyes, often triggered by pollen, dust, or pet dander. Symptoms typically include sneezing and itching more than fever.
precautions: Reduce exposure to triggers when possible (keep windows closed during high pollen periods).
Consider using air filtration and regular cleaning/dusting.
Shower after outdoor exposure to remove pollen from hair/skin.
If symptoms are persistent or severe, a clinician may recommend allergy testing or longer-term treatment.

otc_medicines.name: Non-drowsy antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine)
otc_medicines.how_often: Usually once daily (follow the package label for your specific product).
dosage_guidelines: Adults: follow the package label for dose and frequency.
Many are taken once daily; confirm for your specific product.
If you feel drowsy, avoid driving and consider a different option.

otc_medicines.name: Saline nasal spray
otc_medicines.how_often: As directed on the label (often 1-3 times daily or as needed).
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the label.
Can help rinse allergens from the nasal passages.

otc_medicines.name: Antihistamine eye drops (if itchy/watery eyes)
otc_medicines.how_often: 1 drop in affected eye(s) up to 2-4 times daily (follow the package label).
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed; avoid sharing eye drops.
Stop if you get eye pain or worsening redness and seek care.
Start with one product at a time to know what helps.
Follow label guidance and check for interactions with other medications.
when_to_consult_doctor: Wheezing, shortness of breath, or asthma flare.
Severe eye symptoms (pain, vision changes).
Symptoms not improving after trying OTC options as directed.
Frequent recurrent symptoms that significantly affect daily life.

diseases.name: Strep Throat (Possible)
diseases.description: Strep throat is a bacterial infection that can cause severe sore throat and fever. It often requires a test and antibiotics—OTC medicines can help with pain, but they do not replace evaluation.
precautions: Consider getting a rapid strep test, especially if symptoms are significant.
Avoid close contact if you might be contagious.

otc_medicines.name: Pain/fever relief (acetaminophen or ibuprofen)
otc_medicines.how_often: Choose ONE: acetaminophen every 4-6 hours as needed OR ibuprofen every 6-8 hours as needed - follow the package label.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the package label.
Do not exceed the maximum daily dose stated on the label.
Avoid NSAIDs if contraindicated for you.

otc_medicines.name: Throat soothing (lozenges, warm saltwater gargles)
otc_medicines.how_often: As directed on the label (often every 2-3 hours as needed). Gargles: repeat as needed.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the label.
Warm saltwater gargles can reduce throat discomfort.
OTC medicines are for symptom relief only.
If strep is suspected, seek medical evaluation rather than relying only on OTC treatment.
when_to_consult_doctor: Severe sore throat with fever, especially if there is no cough.
Trouble swallowing, drooling, muffled voice, or trouble breathing.
Signs of dehydration.
Symptoms lasting more than 2-3 days or worsening.

diseases.name: Gastroenteritis / Food Poisoning (Possible)
diseases.description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines, often due to viruses or contaminated food. Common symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and/or diarrhea.
precautions: Focus on hydration: take small sips of oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte fluids.
Avoid alcohol; go easy on heavy/fatty foods at first.
Wash hands thoroughly to reduce spread.

otc_medicines.name: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) / electrolyte drinks
otc_medicines.how_often: Small frequent sips, especially after each loose stool; follow the ORS product instructions.
dosage_guidelines: Use according to product instructions.
Small frequent sips are often easier if nauseated.

otc_medicines.name: Bismuth subsalicylate (for symptom relief, if appropriate)
otc_medicines.how_often: Often up to 4 times/day as needed (follow the label). Avoid if you have aspirin allergy or are told not to take salicylates.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the label.
Avoid if you have aspirin allergy or are advised not to take salicylates.

otc_medicines.name: Acetaminophen for fever/aches (if needed)
otc_medicines.how_often: Every 4-6 hours as needed (adults) - follow the package label.
dosage_guidelines: Use as directed on the package label.
Do not exceed the label maximum daily dose.
For diarrhea, prioritize hydration first.
Avoid anti-diarrheal medicines if you have blood in stool or high fever unless a clinician advises.
when_to_consult_doctor: Blood in stool, black/tarry stool, or severe abdominal pain.
Signs of dehydration (very dry mouth, dizziness, minimal urination).
High fever or symptoms lasting more than 2-3 days.
If you are pregnant, immunocompromised, or a child with concerning symptoms.
